EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing south of Long Island and moving northeastward across southern New England brought six to twelve inches of snow to northern Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire with lesser amounts elsewhere in the region. Much of this fell during the evening rush hour impacting travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to twelve inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving off the mid-Atlantic coast intensified as it moved northeastward over Nantucket. This spread heavy snow across all of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to twelve inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought a brief period of heavy snowfall to southern New Hampshire.  This snowfall was heaviest across eastern Hillsborough County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal storm moved up the east coast and over Nantucket bringing a heavy wet snow to much of the area. This resulted in tree damage and scattered power outages across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.  The heavy wet snow brought down trees, limbs, and wires in Merrimack and Nashua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought six to twelve inches of snow across much of southern New England. Lesser amounts fell east of the Interstate 95 corridor where snow changed to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold air advection behind a mid level low resulted in strong winds at the surface. This caused some wind damage mainly across eastern Hillsborough County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at Manchester Airport (KMHT) recorded sustained winds of 38 mph and gusts to 50 mph.  A tree and wires were downed onto Spring Street in Hillsborough.  A tree and wires were downed blocking the intersection of Brown Avenue and Faith Lane in Manchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant, rapidly developing coastal storm moved southeast of Southern New England bringing heavy snow, bitter cold temperatures, and strong winds to all of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to nine inches of snow fell across eastern Hillsborough County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet coupled with the advance of a cold front resulted in windy conditions at the surface. This led to some wind damage particularly across eastern Hillsborough County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at Manchester Airport (KMHT) recorded wind gusts of 42 mph. A tree was downed onto Burns Hill Road in Hudson, closing the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm intensified very quickly off the east coast of the United States. This storm brought strong winds to parts of New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Chestnut Street in Nashua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying coastal low pressure system moved up the coast from North Carolina, crossing the benchmark (40 N/70 W) southeast of Nantucket. This storm brought strong to damaging winds to much of New England. Little precipitation was received with this storm, with less than 1 to 2 inches of snow reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Dublin Avenue in Nashua. In Merrimack, a tree and wires were downed on Wire Road between Mallard Point and Ichabod Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front produced some scattered convection as it dropped south through the region. One isolated cell became severe producing damaging winds in Belknap County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires in Center Barnstead.

A slow moving frontal boundary, combined with plenty of heat and humidity, led to the development of showers and thunderstorms during the evening. Some of the storms produced torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on Olivo Road in Keene which cut a hole in the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved offshore allowing the environment to become more unstable and allowing afternoon heating to initiate thunderstorms. A few of these storms became severe producing damaging winds along with lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a garage on Fox Run Road setting it on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved offshore allowing the environment to become more unstable and allowing afternoon heating to initiate thunderstorms. A few of these storms became severe producing damaging winds along with lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires on Brook Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid air over southern New England was enough to produce diurnal thunderstorms. A few of these became severe producing both large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires on Dover Street in Keene were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front, combined with tropical moisture in advance of Hurricane Arthur, produced two rounds of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed onto wires on Pepperell Road in Hollis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary, combined with plenty of heat and humidity, led to the development of showers and thunderstorms during the evening. Some of the storms produced torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Keene, flash flooding made Woodland Avenue impassable, and flooding extended into one home. In Winchester, Route 119 by the covered bridge was closed due to flooding, and one side of the road was partially washed out. Old Westport Road was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary, combined with plenty of heat and humidity, led to the development of showers and thunderstorms during the evening. Some of the storms produced torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding washed out portions of Hodgen Road, Center Road, and Abijah Road in North Weare. A spotter reported 2.95 inches of rain in a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across southern New Hampshire ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house on Valley Street in Manchester was struck by lightning, which ignited a fire in the attic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced 2 to 3 inches of rain across northern New Hampshire resulting in minor flooding on the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock. The river was only above flood stage for less than 2 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced 2 to 3 inches of rain resulting in minor flooding on the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock (flood stage 9.0 ft), which crested at 9.41 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance combined with very cold air aloft to produce showers and thunderstorms across much of Southern New England.  Many of these storms produced large hail.

Low pressure passing west of New England set up a modest southerly wind flow gradually increasing seas. The highest astronomical tides of the month combined with the increasing seas to produce minor coastal flooding in the Hampton area. Minor coastal flooding occurred on vulnerable Island Path and Brown Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest astronomical tides of the year combined with rising seas to produce minor coastal flooding in Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A January thaw that lasted from the 13th to the 16th of the month sent temperatures to near 50 degrees over the 3 day period and temperatures remained above freezing for almost 48 hours. In addition 1 to 2 inches of rain fell from the 11th to the 12th with an additional 0.75 to 1.5 inches from the 14th to the 15th. This caused ice to move and jam on the Sugar River at West Claremont, the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock and Plymouth, and on the Saco River at Conway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snow melt and heavy rain caused an ice jam on the Sugar River at West Claremont (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 10.25 ft. This resulted in flooding on Twistback Road and Beauregard Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the Delmarva Peninsula on the morning of the 9th drifted slowly north and northeast through the morning of the 12th.  The system brought 4 to 8 inches of snow to much of Coos County during the afternoon and evening of the 9th before the precipitation changed to rain.

An area of low pressure developed off the Carolina coast  on the morning of the 26th and raced rapidly up the eastern seaboard during the day to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 27th.  The low brought heavy snow to all of New Hampshire with a mixture of precipitation along the coast.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches in the northern mountains to 10 to 15 inches across portions of Sullivan, Grafton, Belknap, and Carroll Counties, to 4 to 8 inches across the southeastern part of the state.

A slow moving cold front spread rain across the region during the evening hours of May 16th. The rain continued through the night and ended by noon on the 17th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High shear and decent moisture and instability in advance of a cold front allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop.  Some of these storms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a car on Westminster Street by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low developed in the eastern Great Lakes, providing enough lift and energy for showers and thunderstorms to develop across southern New England. With plenty of moisture, shear and instability in the region, some of these storms became severe, producing strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime sized hail was reported on Route 3A near Manchester Boston Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front dropped 2 to 5 inches of rain causing minor flooding on the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock and the Saco River at Conway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced minor flooding on the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock (flood stage 9.0 ft), which crested at 11.81 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front dropped 2 to 5 inches of rain causing minor flooding on the Pemigewasset River at Woodstock and the Saco River at Conway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced minor flooding on the Saco River at Conway (flood stage 9.0 ft), which crested at 9.27 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced 2 to 5 inches of rain in less than 6 hours causing flash flooding in Carroll County. Route 122 was washed out as well as several other area roads. A bridge over the Lost River flooded stranding 80 campers at the Lost River Campground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in the Jackson area washing out several roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced 2 to 5 inches of rain in less than 6 hours causing flash flooding in Carroll County. Route 122 was washed out as well as several other area roads. A bridge over the Lost River flooded stranding 80 campers at the Lost River Campground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Woodstock. A bridge over the lost river flooded stranding 80 campers at the Lost River Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front was the focus for convection on the afternoon of the 25th. An isolated cell became severe downing several trees in Merrimack County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Canterbury Road in Chichester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved offshore allowing the environment to become more unstable and allowing afternoon heating to initiate thunderstorms. A few of these storms became severe producing damaging winds along with lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Beaver Lake Drive were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two people were struck by lightning at the Danforth Bay Camping Resort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning at the Danforth Bay Camping Resort in Freedom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 2 to 3 inches of rain in less than 1 hour resulting in several washed out roads in Penacook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Penacook resulting in several washed out roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and tropical air mass was in place across the region ahead of a slow moving cold front on the afternoon of July 2nd. Strong thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and evening producing damaging winds and some reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Littleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid air over southern New England was enough to produce diurnal thunderstorms. A few of these became severe producing both large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires on Route 12A in Surry were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season Nor'easter developed and moved into the Gulf of Maine early on the 14th. This system produced a prolonged period of gale force winds and building seas.|In addition, very high astronomical tides from a super moon combined with the low pressure system to produce minor coastal flooding across isolated locations in coastal New Hampshire. Minor flooding occurred on Island Path and Brown Street in Hampton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An early season Nor'easter combined with astronomically high tides to produce minor coastal flooding in Hampton. Minor flooding occurred on vulnerable Island Path and Brown Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of July 15th.  Precipitable water values over 2 inches and strong deep layer shear combined with high capes to produce numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding were the main threats on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires on Atlantic Avenue in North Hampton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees blocking Route 145 in Pittsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of July 15th.  Precipitable water values over 2 inches and strong deep layer shear combined with high capes to produce numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding were the main threats on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable air mass and strong wind fields were situated across the region on the afternoon of October 8th. An approaching cold front was the trigger for strong convection which quickly organized into bowing line segments as it entered New Hampshire. Damaging winds were reported in numerous locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees in the Meredith area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front provided enough lift in a rather unstable environment to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern New England.  Many of these storms produced strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred in Hollis.  Initial damage was observed in the woods northwest of Eastman Lane where several large trees were downed.  On Eastman Lane, two maple trees were snapped at the base.  Numerous large branches (18 inches in diameter) were downed across Dow Road.  Two large trees were snapped approximately 20 to 30 feet off the ground.  Two large maple trees were downed at the intersection of Dow Road and Jewett Lane.  Several pine and maple trees along Jewett Lane were snapped and downed.  A house was damaged by a tree falling onto it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered afternoon convection in a hot and humid air mass. An isolated cell  produced wind damage in Belknap County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm downed a tree on a cottage in Alton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable air mass and strong wind fields were situated across the region on the afternoon of October 8th. An approaching cold front was the trigger for strong convection which quickly organized into bowing line segments as it entered New Hampshire. Damaging winds were reported in numerous locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a 6-inch diameter tree near Route 112 and Clearbrook Road with strong winds reported at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced 2 to 3 inches of rain resulting in flash flooding in Wentworth New Hampshire where a park was flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced 2 to 3 inches of rain resulting in flash flooding in Wentworth. The Grafton County Sheriff's Office reported a park under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snow melt to produce minor flooding on the Suncook River in North Chichester and on the Sugar River at West Claremont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of melting snow and 1 to 1.5 inches of rain caused minor flooding on the Sugar River at West Claremont (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 7.8 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Ohio Valley and a secondary low developing off the mid-Atlantic coast on the morning of the 5th brought heavy snow to much of the State as it tracked rapidly northeastward during the day.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches with the heaviest amounts in southern areas.

An area of low pressure over the northern Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 12th tracked northeastward to the Carolina coast by the morning of the 13th spreading snow across the region.  The low continued to move slowly northeastward and was near Portland by the morning of the 14th and then moved quickly into the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 15 as another storm approached from the south.  The low brought heavy snow to the entire state with some mixed precipitation in the southeastern part of the state.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 14 inches with a few areas getting more than 15 inches.

An upper level disturbance moved through Southern New England, igniting showers and thunderstorms across the area. Many of these storms produced small to large size hail thanks to very cold temperatures aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground and caused small dents in owners' cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving east from the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 12th and a developing low off the mid-Atlantic coast caused heavy snow across much of western and northern New Hampshire.   Snow and mixed precipitation continued through much of the day on the 13th as the low moved through the Gulf of Maine and into the Canadian Maritimes.  The northern half of the State generally saw between 6 and 18 inches of snow with lesser amounts to the south.  In the southeastern third of the State, the precipitation fell mainly as rain.

Low pressure moved from the Mid-West into southeastern Canada from the 19th through the 21st as a secondary low developed off the New England Coast and moved northeast.  The secondary low brought heavy snow from the evening of the 19th into the morning of the 20th with 6 to 18 inches across much of central New Hampshire.

A very slow moving cold front produced slow moving showers and thunderstorms which resulted in heavy rain and flash flooding across southern New Hampshire and northwestern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell across portions of Cheshire County flooding Roxbury Street, Colonial Drive, and Old Walpole Road in Keene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of melting snow and 1 to 2 inches of rain caused minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester and on the Lamprey River at Newmarket.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rain produced minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at the end of March at 9.34 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail near the town of Hopkinton during the evening of May 25th. A trained spotter and the Concord fire department report hail up to golf ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.50 inch hail on South Road near Hopkinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail near the town of Hopkinton during the evening of May 25th. A trained spotter and the Concord fire department report hail up to golf ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.75 inch hail where Route 9 and 202 join near Hopkinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late night line of showers and thunderstorms ahead of an advancing cold front moved through southern New Hampshire. An isolated severe cell produced wind damage in Sullivan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Reeds Mill Road near Newport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout touched down briefly on Lake Winnipesaukee.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout touched down briefly on Lake Winnipesaukee during the evening of July 4.  The public provided local media with pictures of the waterspout as it formed and dissipated over the lake.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and tropical air mass was in place across the region ahead of a slow moving cold front on the afternoon of July 2nd. Strong thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and evening producing damaging winds and some reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree blocking Interstate 93 near Littleton.

A complex area of low pressure intensified as it slowly moved off the Mid Atlantic coastline on January 2nd. It continued to reorganize (975 mb) and move south of Cape Cod on the 3rd. Gale force northerly winds on January 3rd combined with building|nearshore waves up to 15 feet to produce splash-over and coastal flooding near the time of high tide. Winds switched to the north during the midday high tide on the 3rd. However...the highest astronomical tides of the month, combined with a modest storm surge to impact the coast from Portland south through the New Hampshire Seacoast. ||Many roads were closed due to moderate flooding in Hampton with a foot of water covering the roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A complex area of low pressure intensified as it slowly moved off the Mid Atlantic coastline on January 2nd. It continued to reorganize (975 mb) and move south of Cape Cod on the 3rd. Gale force northerly winds on January 3rd combined with building|nearshore waves up to 15 feet to produce splash-over and coastal flooding near the time of high tide. Winds switched to the north during the midday high tide on the 3rd. However...the highest astronomical tides of the month, combined with a modest storm surge to impact the coast from Portland south through the New Hampshire Seacoast.||Many roads were closed due to moderate flooding in Hampton with a foot of water covering the roadways. Splash-over led flooding of Asheworth Street.

High temperatures near 80 along with 1 to 2 inches of rain caused the remaining snowpack to quickly melt resulting in widespread flooding. Many roads were flooded and washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on small rivers and streams in Errol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass in advance of a cold front produced heavy showers and thunderstorms during the evening of July 4th. Rainfall rates of 2 inches per hour produced flooding along Eastman Brook in Woodstock. Several people were trapped by flood waters for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers and thunderstorms caused flash flooding along Eastman Brook in Woodstock. Several people were trapped by flood waters for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High temperatures near 80 combined with 1 to 2 inches of rain causing the remaining snowpack to quickly melt resulting in widespread flooding in central and northern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on the Connecticut River at West Lebanon (flood stage 18.0 ft), which crested at 21.17 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low developed in the eastern Great Lakes, providing enough lift and energy for showers and thunderstorms to develop across southern New England. With plenty of moisture, shear and instability in the region, some of these storms became severe, producing strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and two wires on Camp Sergeant Road in Merrimack.
